AI.tldr: The paper introduces MUTEX, a framework for detecting toxic spans in Urdu text using XLM RoBERTa with a CRF layer, achieving a 60% F1 score on a new token-level dataset.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the gap in Urdu toxic span detection, particularly the lack of effective tools and token-level annotated datasets, along with challenges like linguistic complexity, code-switching, and morphological variations.
AI.method: MUTEX leverages a multilingual transformer model (XLM RoBERTa) combined with a CRF layer to perform sequence labeling for Urdu toxic span detection. A manually annotated token-level dataset is employed for this purpose.
AI.result: The proposed MUTEX framework achieved a 60% token-level F1 score, serving as the first supervised baseline for toxic span detection in Urdu. Transformer-based models demonstrated effectiveness in dealing with contextual toxicity and linguistic issues.
AI.conclusion: MUTEX provides a critical improvement for Urdu toxic span detection by introducing a robust approach that addresses linguistic challenges with token-level precision.

title: Physics-consistent deep learning for blind aberration recovery in mobile optics
summary: Mobile photography is often limited by complex, lens-specific optical aberrations. While recent deep learning methods approach this as an end-to-end deblurring task, these "black-box" models lack explicit optical modeling and can hallucinate details. Conversely, classical blind deconvolution remains highly unstable. To bridge this gap, we present Lens2Zernike, a deep learning framework that blindly recovers physical optical parameters from a single blurred image. To the best of our knowledge, no prior work has simultaneously integrated supervision across three distinct optical domains. We introduce a novel physics-consistent strategy that explicitly minimizes errors via direct Zernike coefficient regression (z), differentiable physics constraints encompassing both wavefront and point spread function derivations (p), and auxiliary multi-task spatial map predictions (m). Through an ablation study on a ResNet-18 backbone, we demonstrate that our full multi-task framework (z+p+m) yields a 35% improvement over coefficient-only baselines. Crucially, comparative analysis reveals that our approach outperforms two established deep learning methods from previous literature, achieving significantly lower regression errors. Ultimately, we demonstrate that these recovered physical parameters enable stable non-blind deconvolution, providing substantial in-domain improvement on the patented Institute for Digital Molecular Analytics and Science (IDMxS) Mobile Camera Lens Database for restoring diffraction-limited details from severely aberrated mobile captures.

AI.tldr: Lens2Zernike is a deep learning framework for recovering optical parameters from blurred images, combining physics-based modeling and multi-task learning for better deblurring.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of both deep learning-based deblurring methods, which often hallucinate details, and unstable classical blind deconvolution techniques by incorporating explicit optical modeling.
AI.method: The paper introduces Lens2Zernike, which integrates supervision across optical domains by using Zernike coefficient regression, differentiable physics constraints, and multi-task spatial map predictions for precise modeling and deblurring.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates a 35% improvement in accuracy over baseline methods and outperforms prior deep learning techniques, achieving better regression and deblurring results.
AI.conclusion: Lens2Zernike enables stable non-blind deconvolution of mobile images, effectively restoring details from aberrated photographs by incorporating explicit physical modeling, setting a new benchmark for mobile image restoration.

title: Observing and Controlling Features in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action Models (VLAs) have shown remarkable progress towards embodied intelligence. While their architecture partially resembles that of Large Language Models (LLMs), VLAs exhibit higher complexity due to their multi-modal inputs/outputs and often hybrid nature of transformer and diffusion heads. This is part of the reason why insights from mechanistic interpretability in LLMs, which explain how the internal model representations relate to their output behavior, do not trivially transfer to VLA counterparts. In this work, we propose to close this gap by introducing and analyzing two main concepts: feature-observability and feature-controllability. In particular, we first study features that are linearly encoded in representation space, and show how they can be observed by means of a linear classifier. Then, we use a minimal linear intervention grounded in optimal control to accurately place internal representations and steer the VLA's output towards a desired region. Our results show that targeted, lightweight interventions can reliably steer a robot's behavior while preserving closed-loop capabilities. We demonstrate on different VLA architectures ($π_{0.5}$ and OpenVLA) through simulation experiments that VLAs possess interpretable internal structure amenable to online adaptation without fine-tuning, enabling real-time alignment with user preferences and task requirements.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces tools to better understand and control Vision-Language-Action Models (VLAs) by analyzing their internal structures through feature observability and controllability.
AI.motivation: VLAs are becoming essential for embodied intelligence tasks but are more complex than Large Language Models (LLMs) due to their multi-modal and hybrid nature. Existing LLM interpretability insights do not directly apply to VLAs, driving the need for tailored methodologies.
AI.method: The authors developed methods to study ‘feature-observability’ (using linear classifiers to identify internal features) and ‘feature-controllability’ (using minimal linear interventions to influence outputs). They used simulation experiments to validate these methods on different VLA architectures.
AI.result: The techniques enable targeted, lightweight interventions that steer VLA behavior towards desired outcomes while ensuring the robot retains real-time and adaptable capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The findings show that VLAs have interpretable internal structures that can be adapted without fine-tuning, allowing real-time alignment with user preferences and task goals.

title: WaterSIC: information-theoretically (near) optimal linear layer quantization
summary: This paper considers the problem of converting a given dense linear layer to low precision. The tradeoff between compressed length and output discrepancy is analyzed information theoretically (IT). It is shown that a popular GPTQ algorithm may have an arbitrarily large gap to the IT limit. To alleviate this problem, a novel algorithm, termed ''WaterSIC'', is proposed and is shown to be within a rate gap of 0.255 bits to the IT limit, uniformly over all possible covariance matrices of input activations. The key innovation of WaterSIC's is to allocate different quantization rates to different columns (in-features) of the weight matrix, mimicking the classical IT solution known as ''waterfilling''. Applying WaterSIC to the Llama and Qwen family of LLMs establishes new state-of-the-art performance for all quantization rates from 1 to 4 bits.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "WaterSIC," a novel algorithm for converting dense linear layers to low precision, achieving superior performance within a small gap to the theoretical information limit.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve low precision quantization techniques for neural network models, addressing gaps in existing algorithms and achieving better compression-output discrepancy tradeoff.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the compression-output discrepancy tradeoff information theoretically and proposes the WaterSIC algorithm, which adapts quantization rates across weight matrix columns using a strategy inspired by IT's waterfilling solution.
AI.result: WaterSIC achieves a gap of only 0.255 bits to the IT limit, outperforming the popular GPTQ algorithm. It sets new state-of-the-art performance for various quantization rates (1-4 bits) when applied to Llama and Qwen models.
AI.conclusion: WaterSIC is a groundbreaking algorithm for low precision conversion, offering a near-optimal quantization solution and advancing quantization performance in large language models.

title: GCAgent: Enhancing Group Chat Communication through Dialogue Agents System
summary: As a key form in online social platforms, group chat is a popular space for interest exchange or problem-solving, but its effectiveness is often hindered by inactivity and management challenges. While recent large language models (LLMs) have powered impressive one-to-one conversational agents, their seamlessly integration into multi-participant conversations remains unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce GCAgent, an LLM-driven system for enhancing group chats communication with both entertainment- and utility-oriented dialogue agents. The system comprises three tightly integrated modules: Agent Builder, which customizes agents to align with users' interests; Dialogue Manager, which coordinates dialogue states and manage agent invocations; and Interface Plugins, which reduce interaction barriers by three distinct tools. Through extensive experiment, GCAgent achieved an average score of 4.68 across various criteria and was preferred in 51.04\% of cases compared to its base model. Additionally, in real-world deployments over 350 days, it increased message volume by 28.80\%, significantly improving group activity and engagement. Overall, this work presents a practical blueprint for extending LLM-based dialogue agent from one-party chats to multi-party group scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces GCAgent, an LLM-driven system aimed at improving communication and engagement in group chats.
AI.motivation: To tackle inactivity and management challenges in group chats by integrating language models into multi-participant conversations.
AI.method: The system consists of three modules: Agent Builder to customize agents, Dialogue Manager to coordinate interactions, and Interface Plugins to ease usage. It is designed to blend utility and entertainment-focused chat functionalities.
AI.result: GCAgent was rated with an average score of 4.68 across multiple criteria, preferred in 51.04% of cases, and increased message volume by 28.80% during 350 days of real-world use.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a scalable solution for extending language model applications from one-to-one dialogues to multi-party group chat scenarios, showcasing improvement in group activity and engagement.

title: ARC-TGI: Human-Validated Task Generators with Reasoning Chain Templates for ARC-AGI
summary: The Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC-AGI) probes few-shot abstraction and rule induction on small visual grids, but progress is difficult to measure on static collections of hand-authored puzzles due to overfitting, dataset leakage, and memorisation. We introduce ARC-TGI (ARC Task Generators Inventory), an open-source framework for task-family generators: compact Python programs that sample diverse ARC-AGI tasks while preserving a latent rule. ARC-TGI is built around a solver-facing representation: each generated task is paired with natural-language input and transformation reasoning chains and partially evaluated Python code implementing sampling, transformation, and episode construction. Crucially, ARC-TGI supports task-level constraints so that training examples collectively expose the variations needed to infer the underlying rule, a requirement for human-solvable ARC tasks that independent per-example sampling often fails to guarantee. All generators undergo human refinement and local verification to keep both grids and reasoning traces natural and consistent under variation. We release 461 generators covering 180 ARC-Mini tasks, 215 ARC-AGI-1 tasks (200 train, 15 test), and 66 ARC-AGI-2 tasks (55 train, 11 test), enabling scalable dataset sampling and controlled benchmarking.

AI.tldr: The study introduces ARC-TGI, a framework for generating dynamic ARC tasks with a solver-facing representation to overcome challenges in static datasets such as overfitting and memorization.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the limitations of measuring progress in ARC-AGI due to challenges like overfitting, dataset leakage, and memorization in static, hand-authored task collections.
AI.method: The authors developed ARC-TGI, a framework of compact task-family generators written in Python. Generators sample diverse tasks, preserve latent rules, provide reasoning chains, and enforce task-level constraints with natural human refinement and verification.
AI.result: A collection of 461 task generators was created, covering various ARC categories and enabling scalable dataset sampling and controlled benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: ARC-TGI facilitates diverse and dynamic task generation in ARC, overcoming static dataset limitations and aiding in robust task-solving evaluations.

title: How far have we gone in Generative Image Restoration? A study on its capability, limitations and evaluation practices
summary: Generative Image Restoration (GIR) has achieved impressive perceptual realism, but how far have its practical capabilities truly advanced compared with previous methods? To answer this, we present a large-scale study grounded in a new multi-dimensional evaluation pipeline that assesses models on detail, sharpness, semantic correctness, and overall quality. Our analysis covers diverse architectures, including diffusion-based, GAN-based, PSNR-oriented, and general-purpose generation models, revealing critical performance disparities. Furthermore, our analysis uncovers a key evolution in failure modes that signifies a paradigm shift for the perception-oriented low-level vision field. The central challenge is evolving from the previous problem of detail scarcity (under-generation) to the new frontier of detail quality and semantic control (preventing over-generation). We also leverage our benchmark to train a new IQA model that better aligns with human perceptual judgments. Ultimately, this work provides a systematic study of modern generative image restoration models, offering crucial insights that redefine our understanding of their true state and chart a course for future development.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates generative image restoration (GIR) methods for their practical capabilities compared to previous methods using a new multi-dimensional evaluation framework.
AI.motivation: To critically assess the real-world advancements in generative image restoration methods, especially in detail, sharpness, semantic correctness, and overall perceptual quality.
AI.method: A large-scale study with a novel multi-dimensional evaluation pipeline is conducted, covering various GIR architectures and exploring distinct performance disparities and failure modes.
AI.result: It identifies a shift in challenges from detail scarcity to managing detail quality and semantic control, and it introduces a new IQA model more aligned with human perception.
AI.conclusion: This study redefines our understanding of generative image restoration's capabilities and shortcomings, paving the way for future advancements in perception-oriented low-level vision.

title: cuRoboV2: Dynamics-Aware Motion Generation with Depth-Fused Distance Fields for High-DoF Robots
summary: Effective robot autonomy requires motion generation that is safe, feasible, and reactive. Current methods are fragmented: fast planners output physically unexecutable trajectories, reactive controllers struggle with high-fidelity perception, and existing solvers fail on high-DoF systems. We present cuRoboV2, a unified framework with three key innovations: (1) B-spline trajectory optimization that enforces smoothness and torque limits; (2) a GPU-native TSDF/ESDF perception pipeline that generates dense signed distance fields covering the full workspace, unlike existing methods that only provide distances within sparsely allocated blocks, up to 10x faster and in 8x less memory than the state-of-the-art at manipulation scale, with up to 99% collision recall; and (3) scalable GPU-native whole-body computation, namely topology-aware kinematics, differentiable inverse dynamics, and map-reduce self-collision, that achieves up to 61x speedup while also extending to high-DoF humanoids (where previous GPU implementations fail). On benchmarks, cuRoboV2 achieves 99.7% success under 3kg payload (where baselines achieve only 72--77%), 99.6% collision-free IK on a 48-DoF humanoid (where prior methods fail entirely), and 89.5% retargeting constraint satisfaction (vs. 61% for PyRoki); these collision-free motions yield locomotion policies with 21% lower tracking error than PyRoki and 12x lower cross-seed variance than mink. A ground-up codebase redesign for discoverability enabled LLM coding assistants to author up to 73% of new modules, including hand-optimized CUDA kernels, demonstrating that well-structured robotics code can unlock productive human--LLM collaboration. Together, these advances provide a unified, dynamics-aware motion generation stack that scales from single-arm manipulators to full humanoids.

AI.tldr: cuRoboV2 is a unified framework for motion generation in robotics, offering GPU-optimized trajectory computation, advanced perception pipelines, and scalable whole-body dynamics, delivering significant performance improvements and enhanced results in high-DoF systems.
AI.motivation: The need for a unified robotics motion generation framework arises from limitations in current methods: fragmented functionality, inefficiency with high-DoF systems, and poor integration between planning, control, and perception.
AI.method: cuRoboV2 introduces three innovations: B-spline trajectory optimization, fast GPU-native TSDF/ESDF perception pipelines, and scalable, GPU-optimized whole-body computations including topology-aware kinematics and differentiable dynamics.
AI.result: cuRoboV2 demonstrates strong benchmarks, including 99.7% success under heavy payloads, 99.6% collision-free inverse kinematics in complex humanoids, and improved locomotion policies with reduced tracking errors and variances.
AI.conclusion: Through significant advancements in processing speed, memory efficiency, and collision handling, cuRoboV2 creates a unified and scalable robotics motion generation stack, enabling high-DoF robots to perform safely and efficiently.

title: Mixture of Universal Experts: Scaling Virtual Width via Depth-Width Transformation
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decouples model capacity from per-token computation, yet their scalability remains limited by the physical dimensions of depth and width. To overcome this, we propose Mixture of Universal Experts (MOUE),a MoE generalization introducing a novel scaling dimension: Virtual Width. In general, MoUE aims to reuse a universal layer-agnostic expert pool across layers, converting depth into virtual width under a fixed per-token activation budget. However, two challenges remain: a routing path explosion from recursive expert reuse, and a mismatch between the exposure induced by reuse and the conventional load-balancing objectives. We address these with three core components: a Staggered Rotational Topology for structured expert sharing, a Universal Expert Load Balance for depth-aware exposure correction, and a Universal Router with lightweight trajectory state for coherent multi-step routing. Empirically, MoUE consistently outperforms matched MoE baselines by up to 1.3% across scaling regimes, enables progressive conversion of existing MoE checkpoints with up to 4.2% gains, and reveals a new scaling dimension for MoE architectures.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Mixture of Universal Experts (MoUE), expanding scalability by reusing universal experts across layers with virtual width.
AI.motivation: To address scalability limitations in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures caused by depth and width constraints.
AI.method: MoUE introduces universal expert reuse across layers with structured sharing (Staggered Rotational Topology), new load balancing (Universal Expert Load Balance), and adaptive routing (Universal Router).
AI.result: MoUE outperforms traditional MoE benchmarks by up to 1.3%, and enables progressive conversion with gains up to 4.2%.
AI.conclusion: MoUE provides a novel scaling dimension for MoE architectures, improving efficiency and performance.

title: X-RAY: Mapping LLM Reasoning Capability via Formalized and Calibrated Probes
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve promising performance, yet their ability to reason remains poorly understood. Existing evaluations largely emphasize task-level accuracy, often conflating pattern matching with reasoning capability. We present X-RAY, an explainable reasoning analysis system that maps the LLM reasoning capability using calibrated, formally verified probes. We model reasoning capability as a function of extractable \textit{structure}, operationalized through formal properties such as constraint interaction, reasoning depth, and solution-space geometry. X-Ray generates probes via formal tools with controlled structural variations, enabling precise isolation of incremental structural information through formal calibration and verification. We evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on problems ranging from junior-level to advanced in mathematics, physics, and chemistry. Our analysis reveals a systematic asymmetry in LLM reasoning: models are relatively robust to constraint refinement, where additional conditions shrink an existing solution space, but degrade sharply under solution-space restructuring, where modifications alter the underlying structural form of the solution manifold. Moreover, calibrated formal probes differentiate models that appear indistinguishable on standard benchmarks and reveal failure modes that are structurally interpretable rather than opaque. Beyond evaluation, our framework is contamination-free and supports the training and testing of reasoning models.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes Large Language Models' reasoning abilities using X-RAY, a system employing formally verified probes to systematically evaluate and map reasoning capability.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of understanding of reasoning capabilities in LLMs and provide structural insights, rather than just task-level accuracy.
AI.method: X-RAY evaluates LLMs using calibrated probes designed with formal tools that isolate structural reasoning properties such as constraint interaction and solution-space geometry.
AI.result: LLMs exhibit asymmetry in reasoning abilities, being robust to constraint refinement but struggling with solution-space restructuring. Calibrated probes reveal key differences in LLM capabilities and structural failure modes.
AI.conclusion: X-RAY provides contamination-free evaluations of LLM reasoning, enabling interpretable failure analysis, differentiation between models, and potential training/test improvements in reasoning tasks.

title: Measuring the Redundancy of Decoder Layers in SpeechLLMs
summary: Speech Large Language Models route speech encoder representations into an LLM decoder that typically accounts for over 90% of total parameters. We study how much of this decoder capacity is actually needed for speech tasks. Across two LLM families and three scales (1-8B), we show that decoder redundancy is largely inherited from the pretrained LLM: text and speech inputs yield similar redundant blocks. We then measure excess capacity by pruning decoder layers and analysing post-pruning healing to increase robustness. Our findings show that 7-8B models retain good ASR performance with only 60% of decoder layers, and the same trend extends to smaller scales with reduced pruning tolerance. We then generalise to speech translation, and show that the same blocks of layers are redundant across speech encoders, tasks and languages, indicating that a more global redundancy structure exists, enabling a single pruned and multi-tasks SpeechLLM backbone to be deployed.

AI.tldr: The paper explores decoder redundancy in Speech Large Language Models (SpeechLLMs) and finds that a significant portion of decoder layers is unnecessary without compromising performance, enabling a more effective multi-task backbone.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand and optimize the usage of decoder layers in SpeechLLMs by studying redundancy, with the goal of improving computational efficiency for speech tasks.
AI.method: The authors analyze redundancy in decoders of different LLM families at multiple scales, apply layer pruning, and evaluate post-pruning performance to determine excess capacity and adaptability.
AI.result: Findings reveal that large SpeechLLMs can achieve strong ASR performance with only 60% of decoder layers. This redundancy extends across tasks, speech encoders, and languages, highlighting global redundancy in the backbone.
AI.conclusion: A substantial portion of the decoder in SpeechLLMs is redundant, allowing for a single pruned, multi-task SpeechLLM design, which is both efficient and effective across tasks and languages.

title: Tell2Adapt: A Unified Framework for Source Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation via Vision Foundation Model
comment: Accepted by IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR 2026)
summary: Source Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (SFUDA) is critical for deploying deep learning models across diverse clinical settings. However, existing methods are typically designed for low-gap, specific domain shifts and cannot generalize into a unified, multi-modalities, and multi-target framework, which presents a major barrier to real-world application. To overcome this issue, we introduce Tell2Adapt, a novel SFUDA framework that harnesses the vast, generalizable knowledge of the Vision Foundation Model (VFM). Our approach ensures high-fidelity VFM prompts through Context-Aware Prompts Regularization (CAPR), which robustly translates varied text prompts into canonical instructions. This enables the generation of high-quality pseudo-labels for efficiently adapting the lightweight student model to target domain. To guarantee clinical reliability, the framework incorporates Visual Plausibility Refinement (VPR), which leverages the VFM's anatomical knowledge to re-ground the adapted model's predictions in target image's low-level visual features, effectively removing noise and false positives. We conduct one of the most extensive SFUDA evaluations to date, validating our framework across 10 domain adaptation directions and 22 anatomical targets, including brain, cardiac, polyp, and abdominal targets. Our results demonstrate that Tell2Adapt consistently outperforms existing approaches, achieving SOTA for a unified SFUDA framework in medical image segmentation. Code are avaliable at https://github.com/derekshiii/Tell2Adapt.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Tell2Adapt, a novel framework in Source-Free Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (SFUDA) for medical image segmentation, leveraging Vision Foundation Models to enhance adaptability across diverse domains.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of existing SFUDA methods that fail to generalize across different domain shifts, modalities, and targets, which hinders their real-world applicability in diverse clinical settings.
AI.method: The proposed method integrates a Vision Foundation Model with Context-Aware Prompts Regularization (CAPR) for generating accurate prompts and Visual Plausibility Refinement (VPR) for grounding predictions in target visual features, minimizing noise and false positives.
AI.result: The extensive evaluation of Tell2Adapt across 10 domain adaptation directions and 22 anatomical targets highlights its superior performance over existing methods for medical image segmentation.
AI.conclusion: Tell2Adapt effectively addresses the challenges in SFUDA, offering a unified, robust solution and achieving state-of-the-art performance, paving the way for broader clinical deployment.

title: Safe-SAGE: Social-Semantic Adaptive Guidance for Safe Engagement through Laplace-Modulated Poisson Safety Functions
summary: Traditional safety-critical control methods, such as control barrier functions, suffer from semantic blindness, exhibiting the same behavior around obstacles regardless of contextual significance. This limitation leads to the uniform treatment of all obstacles, despite their differing semantic meanings. We present Safe-SAGE (Social-Semantic Adaptive Guidance for Safe Engagement), a unified framework that bridges the gap between high-level semantic understanding and low-level safety-critical control through a Poisson safety function (PSF) modulated using a Laplace guidance field. Our approach perceives the environment by fusing multi-sensor point clouds with vision-based instance segmentation and persistent object tracking to maintain up-to-date semantics beyond the camera's field of view. A multi-layer safety filter is then used to modulate system inputs to achieve safe navigation using this semantic understanding of the environment. This safety filter consists of both a model predictive control layer and a control barrier function layer. Both layers utilize the PSF and flux modulation of the guidance field to introduce varying levels of conservatism and multi-agent passing norms for different obstacles in the environment. Our framework enables legged robots to navigate semantically rich, dynamic environments with context-dependent safety margins while maintaining rigorous safety guarantees.

AI.tldr: Safe-SAGE integrates semantic understanding with low-level control to allow robots to navigate complex environments safely using a pioneering safety filter framework.
AI.motivation: Traditional safety-critical methods like control barrier functions inadequately account for the contextual meaning of obstacles, treating all obstacles uniformly.
AI.method: Safe-SAGE uses a Poisson safety function and Laplace guidance field combined with sensor fusion, vision-based semantic segmentation, object tracking, and a multi-layer safety filter involving model predictive control and control barrier functions.
AI.result: Safe-SAGE successfully enables legged robots to navigate dynamic and semantically complex environments with adaptive safety margins dictated by context.
AI.conclusion: The framework bridges semantic interpretation with rigorous control systems, demonstrating enhanced safety and adaptability for legged robots in complex settings.

title: Functionality-Oriented LLM Merging on the Fisher--Rao Manifold
summary:
Weight-space merging aims to combine multiple fine-tuned LLMs into a single model without retraining, yet most existing approaches remain fundamentally parameter-space heuristics. This creates three practical limitations. First, linear averaging, task vectors, and related rules operate on Euclidean coordinates, even though the desired goal is to merge functionality, i.e., predictive behaviors across tasks. Second, when the source checkpoints are farther apart or more heterogeneous, Euclidean blends often trigger representation collapse, manifested as activation variance shrinkage and effective-rank degradation, which sharply degrades accuracy. Third, many geometry-inspired methods are most natural for two-model interpolation and do not extend cleanly to merging N>2 experts with a principled objective.
  We address these issues by formulating model merging as computing a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher--Rao manifold, which is locally equivalent to minimizing a KL-based function distance between predictive distributions. We derive a practical fixed-point algorithm using a lightweight spherical proxy that preserves norms and generalizes directly to multi-expert merging. Across various benchmarks and collapse diagnostics, our method remains stable as the number and heterogeneity of merged models increase, consistently outperforming prior baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method for merging fine-tuned models by using a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher--Rao manifold to address shortcomings of traditional Euclidean-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for merging fine-tuned large language models often fail due to their reliance on Euclidean parameter-space heuristics, leading to problems like representation collapse and limited scalability with multiple models.
AI.method: The proposed method uses a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher--Rao manifold, which minimizes KL-based functional distance between predictive distributions. A fixed-point algorithm with a spherical proxy enables stable merging of multiple heterogeneous models.
AI.result: The method successfully maintains activation variance and effective rank while outperforming prior baselines in multi-expert merging scenarios across various benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Merging fine-tuned models via Fisher--Rao manifold methods offers superior stability and accuracy, especially as model heterogeneity and number increase, overcoming traditional limitations of parameter-space approaches.

title: STRUCTUREDAGENT: Planning with AND/OR Trees for Long-Horizon Web Tasks
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems for sequential decision-making. Such agents must perceive their environment, reason across multiple time steps, and take actions that optimize long-term objectives. However, existing web agents struggle on complex, long-horizon tasks due to limited in-context memory for tracking history, weak planning abilities, and greedy behaviors that lead to premature termination. To address these challenges, we propose STRUCTUREDAGENT, a hierarchical planning framework with two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner that uses dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search and (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction in information-seeking tasks. The framework also produces interpretable hierarchical plans, enabling easier debugging and facilitating human intervention when needed. Our results on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks show that STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces STRUCTUREDAGENT, a hierarchical planning framework that enhances large language model (LLM)-based agents' ability to perform complex, long-horizon web tasks by using dynamic AND/OR trees and structured memory modules.
AI.motivation: Existing web agents face limitations in handling complex, long-horizon tasks due to weak planning, limited memory, and greedy decision-making, necessitating an improved approach for long-term optimization.
AI.method: STRUCTUREDAGENT combines a hierarchical planner based on dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search and a structured memory module to track and improve solutions in information-seeking tasks.
AI.result: STRUCTUREDAGENT outperforms standard LLM-based agents in long-horizon web-browsing tasks across benchmarks like WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework improves web agent performance by addressing key limitations in memory, planning, and task execution, offering interpretable plans for better debugging and collaboration.

title: LBM: Hierarchical Large Auto-Bidding Model via Reasoning and Acting
summary: The growing scale of ad auctions on online advertising platforms has intensified competition, making manual bidding impractical and necessitating auto-bidding to help advertisers achieve their economic goals. Current auto-bidding methods have evolved to use offline reinforcement learning or generative methods to optimize bidding strategies, but they can sometimes behave counterintuitively due to the black-box training manner and limited mode coverage of datasets, leading to challenges in understanding task status and generalization in dynamic ad environments. Large language models (LLMs) offer a promising solution by leveraging prior human knowledge and reasoning abilities to improve auto-bidding performance. However, directly applying LLMs to auto-bidding faces difficulties due to the need for precise actions in competitive auctions and the lack of specialized auto-bidding knowledge, which can lead to hallucinations and suboptimal decisions. To address these challenges, we propose a hierarchical Large autoBidding Model (LBM) to leverage the reasoning capabilities of LLMs for developing a superior auto-bidding strategy. This includes a high-level LBM-Think model for reasoning and a low-level LBM-Act model for action generation. Specifically, we propose a dual embedding mechanism to efficiently fuse two modalities, including language and numerical inputs, for language-guided training of the LBM-Act; then, we propose an offline reinforcement fine-tuning technique termed GQPO for mitigating the LLM-Think's hallucinations and enhancing decision-making performance without simulation or real-world rollout like previous multi-turn LLM-based methods. Experiments demonstrate the superiority of a generative backbone based on our LBM, especially in an efficient training manner and generalization ability.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hierarchical model (LBM) using Large Language Models (LLMs) to improve auto-bidding strategies in ad auctions, overcoming traditional limitations.
AI.motivation: The increasing complexity and competition in ad auctions demand sophisticated auto-bidding approaches. Current methods face challenges due to their black-box nature, limited data coverage, and lack of adaptability to dynamic environments.
AI.method: The proposed LBM framework includes a high-level reasoning model (LBM-Think) and a low-level action generation model (LBM-Act). It integrates dual embedding for combining linguistic and numerical inputs and employs reinforcement fine-tuning (GQPO) to reduce hallucinations and improve decision-making.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate LBM's superiority in terms of efficient training, generalization, and performance compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: The LBM framework proves to be a promising solution to enhance auto-bidding efficiency, adaptability, and decision-making in online ad platforms, leveraging the capabilities of LLMs.

title: Generalizable Multiscale Segmentation of Heterogeneous Map Collections
summary: Historical map collections are highly diverse in style, scale, and geographic focus, often consisting of many single-sheet documents. Yet most work in map recognition focuses on specialist models tailored to homogeneous map series. In contrast, this article aims to develop generalizable semantic segmentation models and ontology. First, we introduce Semap, a new open benchmark dataset comprising 1,439 manually annotated patches designed to reflect the variety of historical map documents. Second, we present a segmentation framework that combines procedural data synthesis with multiscale integration to improve robustness and transferability. This framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on both the HCMSSD and Semap datasets, showing that a diversity-driven approach to map recognition is not only viable but also beneficial. The results indicate that segmentation performance remains largely stable across map collections, scales, geographic regions, and publication contexts. By proposing benchmark datasets and methods for the generic segmentation of historical maps, this work opens the way to integrating the long tail of cartographic archives to historical geographic studies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Semap, a benchmark dataset for historical map segmentation, and a segmentation framework that enhances performance and generalizability, demonstrating the viability of a diversity-driven approach.
AI.motivation: Existing map recognition focuses on homogeneous map series, overlooking the diversity in historical map collections. The aim is to create a generalizable approach to segment diverse historical maps.
AI.method: A benchmark dataset, Semap, is introduced. The segmentation framework incorporates procedural data synthesis and multiscale integration to enhance robustness and transferability.
AI.result: The framework achieves state-of-the-art results on HCMSSD and Semap datasets, maintaining stable segmentation performance across diverse map collections.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates the benefits of a diversity-driven segmentation approach, facilitating the integration of varied cartographic records into historical research.

title: RoboPocket: Improve Robot Policies Instantly with Your Phone
summary: Scaling imitation learning is fundamentally constrained by the efficiency of data collection. While handheld interfaces have emerged as a scalable solution for in-the-wild data acquisition, they predominantly operate in an open-loop manner: operators blindly collect demonstrations without knowing the underlying policy's weaknesses, leading to inefficient coverage of critical state distributions. Conversely, interactive methods like DAgger effectively address covariate shift but rely on physical robot execution, which is costly and difficult to scale. To reconcile this trade-off, we introduce RoboPocket, a portable system that enables Robot-Free Instant Policy Iteration using single consumer smartphones. Its core innovation is a Remote Inference framework that visualizes the policy's predicted trajectory via Augmented Reality (AR) Visual Foresight. This immersive feedback allows collectors to proactively identify potential failures and focus data collection on the policy's weak regions without requiring a physical robot. Furthermore, we implement an asynchronous Online Finetuning pipeline that continuously updates the policy with incoming data, effectively closing the learning loop in minutes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that RoboPocket adheres to data scaling laws and doubles the data efficiency compared to offline scaling strategies, overcoming their long-standing efficiency bottleneck. Moreover, our instant iteration loop also boosts sample efficiency by up to 2$\times$ in distributed environments a small number of interactive corrections per person. Project page and videos: https://robo-pocket.github.io.

AI.tldr: RoboPocket is a system that enables efficient data collection and improvement for imitation learning without using physical robots. It combines augmented reality visual feedback and asynchronous online fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: To address inefficiencies in data collection for imitation learning, particularly to avoid the open-loop limitations of handheld interface methods and the costliness of robot-dependent methods.
AI.method: Introduced RoboPocket, a portable system using smartphones, incorporating augmented reality-based policy trajectory visualization and asynchronous online finetuning.
AI.result: RoboPocket greatly enhances data collection efficiency, doubling it compared to traditional offline methods, and boosts sample efficiency in distributed settings.
AI.conclusion: RoboPocket resolves significant barriers in scalable imitation learning data acquisition, offering an efficient, portable, and robot-free method.

title: Lightweight and Scalable Transfer Learning Framework for Load Disaggregation
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring (NILM) aims to estimate appliance-level consumption from aggregate electrical signals recorded at a single measurement point. In recent years, the field has increasingly adopted deep learning approaches; however, cross-domain generalization remains a persistent challenge due to variations in appliance characteristics, usage patterns, and background loads across homes. Transfer learning provides a practical paradigm to adapt models with limited target data. However, existing methods often assume a fixed appliance set, lack flexibility for evolving real-world deployments, remain unsuitable for edge devices, or scale poorly for real-time operation. This paper proposes RefQuery, a scalable multi-appliance, multi-task NILM framework that conditions disaggregation on compact appliance fingerprints, allowing one shared model to serve many appliances without a fixed output set. RefQuery keeps a pretrained disaggregation network fully frozen and adapts to a target home by learning only a per-appliance embedding during a lightweight backpropagation stage. Experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that RefQuery delivers a strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off against single-appliance and multi-appliance baselines, including modern Transformer-based methods. These results support RefQuery as a practical path toward scalable, real-time NILM on resource-constrained edge devices.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RefQuery, a scalable NILM framework enabling efficient appliance-level disaggregation with flexible adaptation capabilities for edge device deployment.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address scalability, efficiency, and generalization challenges in NILM for real-world applications, especially on edge devices.
AI.method: The proposed RefQuery framework uses a pretrained disaggregation network and learns per-appliance embeddings in a lightweight manner to handle multiple appliances.
AI.result: RefQuery achieves competitive accuracy and efficiency compared to existing methods across three public datasets, showcasing its adaptability and scalability.
AI.conclusion: RefQuery presents a promising pathway for scalable and real-time NILM deployment on resource-constrained devices, overcoming traditional limitations.

title: WebChain: A Large-Scale Human-Annotated Dataset of Real-World Web Interaction Traces
summary: We introduce WebChain, the largest open-source dataset of human-annotated trajectories on real-world websites, designed to accelerate reproducible research in web agents. It contains 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps, featuring a core Triple Alignment of visual, structural, and action data to provide rich, multi-modal supervision. The data is collected via a scalable pipeline that ensures coverage of complex, high-value tasks often missed by synthetic methods. Leveraging this dataset, we propose a Dual Mid-Training recipe that decouples spatial grounding from planning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on our proposed WebChainBench and other public GUI benchmarks. Our work provides the data and insights necessary to build and rigorously evaluate the next generation of scalable web agents.

AI.tldr: WebChain is a large, open-source dataset designed for developing and evaluating web agents, containing human-annotated trajectories and steps with multi-modal visual, structural, and action data.
AI.motivation: To enhance reproducible research and development of scalable web agents by offering a rich dataset that addresses limitations of synthetic methods.
AI.method: The dataset is collected via a scalable pipeline to cover complex tasks. A Dual Mid-Training strategy is introduced to separate spatial grounding from planning.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance achieved on WebChainBench and other public GUI benchmarks using the new dataset and method.
AI.conclusion: WebChain enables the advancement of web agents by providing comprehensive data and a methodology for rigorous evaluation on complex tasks.

title: Representation Fidelity:Auditing Algorithmic Decisions About Humans Using Self-Descriptions
summary: This paper introduces a new dimension for validating algorithmic decisions about humans by measuring the fidelity of their representations. Representation Fidelity measures if decisions about a person rest on reasonable grounds. We propose to operationalize this notion by measuring the distance between two representations of the same person: (1) an externally prescribed input representation on which the decision is based, and (2) a self-description provided by the human subject of the decision, used solely to validate the input representation. We examine the nature of discrepancies between these representations, how such discrepancies can be quantified, and derive a generic typology of representation mismatches that determine the degree of representation fidelity. We further present the first benchmark for evaluating representation fidelity based on a dataset of loan-granting decisions. Our Loan-Granting Self-Representations Corpus 2025 consists of a large corpus of 30 000 synthetic natural language self-descriptions derived from corresponding representations of applicants in the German Credit Dataset, along with expert annotations of representation mismatches between each pair of representations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 'Representation Fidelity' to validate decisions by comparing external input representation and self-description of individuals.
AI.motivation: To ensure decisions about humans are made on reasonable grounds by assessing representation mismatches.
AI.method: The study operationalizes Representation Fidelity by quantifying the differences between external and self-provided descriptions, proposes a typology of mismatches, and introduces a benchmark dataset for evaluation.
AI.result: A corpus of 30,000 synthetic self-descriptions linked to loan decisions in the German Credit Dataset, along with expert annotations of mismatches.
AI.conclusion: Representation Fidelity can help enhance the fairness and reliability of algorithmic decisions about individuals.

title: Exploiting Intermediate Reconstructions in Optical Coherence Tomography for Test-Time Adaption of Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Primary health care frequently relies on low-cost imaging devices, which are commonly used for screening purposes. To ensure accurate diagnosis, these systems depend on advanced reconstruction algorithms designed to approximate the performance of high-quality counterparts. Such algorithms typically employ iterative reconstruction methods that incorporate domain-specific prior knowledge. However, downstream task performance is generally assessed using only the final reconstructed image, thereby disregarding the informative intermediate representations generated throughout the reconstruction process. In this work, we propose IRTTA to exploit these intermediate representations at test-time by adapting the normalization-layer parameters of a frozen downstream network via a modulator network that conditions on the current reconstruction timescale. The modulator network is learned during test-time using an averaged entropy loss across all individual timesteps. Variation among the timestep-wise segmentations additionally provides uncertainty estimates at no extra cost. This approach enhances segmentation performance and enables semantically meaningful uncertainty estimation, all without modifying either the reconstruction process or the downstream model.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces IRTTA, a method for leveraging intermediate representations from iterative reconstruction processes in low-cost imaging devices to improve downstream tasks like segmentation and uncertainty estimation.
AI.motivation: To address the limitation of using only final reconstructed images from low-cost imaging devices for downstream tasks, thus ignoring valuable intermediate reconstruction data.
AI.method: IRTTA adapts normalization-layer parameters of a downstream network during test-time by learning a modulator network, which uses entropy loss over intermediate reconstruction timesteps to improve segmentation and estimate uncertainty.
AI.result: IRTTA enhances segmentation performance and provides uncertainty estimates based on timestep variations without requiring any changes to the reconstruction process or the downstream model.
AI.conclusion: Using intermediate representations and a modulator network at test-time can significantly improve downstream task performance (segmentation and uncertainty estimation) for low-cost imaging devices.

title: Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative Control Strategy for Alleviating Mixed Traffic Congestion
summary: As Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) develops, Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) are expected to significantly reduce traffic congestion through cooperative strategies, such as in bottleneck areas. However, the uncertainty and diversity in the behaviors of Human-Driven Vehicles (HDVs) in mixed traffic environments present major challenges for CAV cooperation. This paper proposes a Dual-Interaction-Aware Cooperative Control (DIACC) strategy that enhances both local and global interaction perception within the Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework for Connected and Automated Vehicles (CAVs) in mixed traffic bottleneck scenarios. The DIACC strategy consists of three key innovations: 1) A Decentralized Interaction-Adaptive Decision-Making (D-IADM) module that enhances actor's local interaction perception by distinguishing CAV-CAV cooperative interactions from CAV-HDV observational interactions. 2) A Centralized Interaction-Enhanced Critic (C-IEC) that improves critic's global traffic understanding through interaction-aware value estimation, providing more accurate guidance for policy updates. 3) A reward design that employs softmin aggregation with temperature annealing to prioritize interaction-intensive scenarios in mixed traffic. Additionally, a lightweight Proactive Safety-based Action Refinement (PSAR) module applies rule-based corrections to accelerate training convergence. Experimental results demonstrate that DIACC significantly improves traffic efficiency and adaptability compared to rule-based and benchmark MARL models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the DIACC strategy to enhance cooperation among CAVs in mixed traffic bottleneck scenarios using a MARL framework, which significantly improves traffic efficiency.
AI.motivation: Challenges arise for CAV cooperation due to uncertainties and varied behaviors of Human-Driven Vehicles (HDVs) in mixed traffic environments, particularly in bottlenecks.
AI.method: The proposed strategy, DIACC, incorporates a Decentralized Interaction-Adaptive Decision-Making module, a Centralized Interaction-Enhanced Critic, a reward design with temperature annealing, and a Proactive Safety-based Action Refinement module within the MARL framework.
AI.result: Experimental results show that DIACC outperforms both rule-based models and benchmark MARL frameworks in traffic efficiency and adaptability.
AI.conclusion: DIACC effectively enhances CAV cooperation in mixed traffic scenarios, addressing HDV behavior uncertainty and improving traffic dynamics.

title: Competitive Multi-Operator Reinforcement Learning for Joint Pricing and Fleet Rebalancing in AMoD Systems
summary: Autonomous Mobility-on-Demand (AMoD) systems promise to revolutionize urban transportation by providing affordable on-demand services to meet growing travel demand. However, realistic AMoD markets will be competitive, with multiple operators competing for passengers through strategic pricing and fleet deployment. While reinforcement learning has shown promise in optimizing single-operator AMoD control, existing work fails to capture competitive market dynamics. We investigate the impact of competition on policy learning by introducing a multi-operator reinforcement learning framework where two operators simultaneously learn pricing and fleet rebalancing policies. By integrating discrete choice theory, we enable passenger allocation and demand competition to emerge endogenously from utility-maximizing decisions. Experiments using real-world data from multiple cities demonstrate that competition fundamentally alters learned behaviors, leading to lower prices and distinct fleet positioning patterns compared to monopolistic settings. Notably, we demonstrate that learning-based approaches are robust to the additional stochasticity of competition, with competitive agents successfully converging to effective policies while accounting for partially unobserved competitor strategies.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the impact of competition in Autonomous Mobility-on-Demand systems by introducing a reinforcement learning framework for multi-operator scenarios. Results show competition affects policies, reducing prices and altering fleet management.
AI.motivation: Autonomous Mobility-on-Demand systems have high potential for transforming urban transportation, but current research falls short of addressing competitive dynamics in multi-operator markets.
AI.method: The paper proposes a framework where two operators use reinforcement learning to simultaneously optimize pricing and fleet rebalancing, incorporating discrete choice theory for endogenous passenger demand allocation.
AI.result: Experiments using real-world urban data demonstrate competition drives lower pricing and distinct fleet adjustments. The reinforcement learning-based approaches effectively handle competition-induced stochasticity.
AI.conclusion: Competition significantly influences AMoD system policies, and learning-based strategies remain robust, showing potential for application in competitive transport markets.

title: UniSTOK: Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging
summary: Spatio-temporal kriging aims to infer signals at unobserved locations from observed sensors and is critical to applications such as transportation and environmental monitoring. In practice, however, observed sensors themselves often exhibit heterogeneous missingness, forcing inductive kriging models to rely on crudely imputed inputs. This setting brings three key challenges: (1) it is unclear whether an value is a true signal or a missingness-induced artifact; (2) missingness is highly heterogeneous across sensors and time; (3) missing observations distort the local spatio-temporal structure. To address these issues, we propose Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging (UniSTOK), a plug-and-play framework that enhances existing inductive kriging backbones under missing observation. Our framework forms a dual-branch input consisting of the original observations and a jigsaw-augmented counterpart that synthesizes proxy signals only at missing entries. The two branches are then processed in parallel by a shared spatio-temporal backbone with explicit missingness mask modulation. Their outputs are finally adaptively fused via dual-channel attention. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets under diverse missing patterns demonstrate consistent and significant improvements.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging (UniSTOK) to handle missing data in spatio-temporal tasks, improving model performance with a dual-branch structure and adaptive attention mechanisms.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in spatio-temporal kriging when sensor data is missing, which distorts predictions, leading to inaccurate modeling in applications like transportation and environmental monitoring.
AI.method: UniSTOK introduces a dual-branch system: one processing original observations and the other synthesizing proxy signals for missing entries. These branches use a shared backbone with missingness modulation and are fused using dual-channel attention.
AI.result: UniSTOK significantly outperforms existing spatio-temporal kriging models on real-world datasets with diverse missing patterns.
AI.conclusion: UniSTOK effectively enhances kriging models' robustness, making them more accurate and reliable under varying missing data scenarios.

title: Feature Resemblance: On the Theoretical Understanding of Analogical Reasoning in Transformers
summary: Understanding reasoning in large language models is complicated by evaluations that conflate multiple reasoning types. We isolate analogical reasoning (inferring shared properties between entities based on known similarities) and analyze its emergence in transformers. We theoretically prove three key results: (1) Joint training on similarity and attribution premises enables analogical reasoning through aligned representations; (2) Sequential training succeeds only when similarity structure is learned before specific attributes, revealing a necessary curriculum; (3) Two-hop reasoning ($a \to b, b \to c \implies a \to c$) reduces to analogical reasoning with identity bridges ($b = b$), which must appear explicitly in training data. These results reveal a unified mechanism: transformers encode entities with similar properties into similar representations, enabling property transfer through feature alignment. Experiments with architectures up to 1.5B parameters validate our theory and demonstrate how representational geometry shapes inductive reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper studies analogical reasoning in transformers, proposing that aligned representations enable property transfer through similarity and demonstrating theoretical and experimental validation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand reasoning in large language models, particularly focusing on isolated analogical reasoning and its emergence in transformers.
AI.method: The authors developed theoretical proofs addressing three key results and validated their findings through experiments with models up to 1.5B parameters.
AI.result: The results reveal that transformers encode entities with shared properties into similar representations, enabling analogical reasoning. A curriculum approach and explicit identity bridges in training data facilitate reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that analogical reasoning in transformers is enabled by representational similarity and can be optimized via structured training approaches. This sheds light on how representational geometry influences reasoning.

title: CoIn3D: Revisiting Configuration-Invariant Multi-Camera 3D Object Detection
summary: Multi-camera 3D object detection (MC3D) has attracted increasing attention with the growing deployment of multi-sensor physical agents, such as robots and autonomous vehicles. However, MC3D models still struggle to generalize to unseen platforms with new multi-camera configurations. Current solutions simply employ a meta-camera for unified representation but lack comprehensive consideration. In this paper, we revisit this issue and identify that the devil lies in spatial prior discrepancies across source and target configurations, including different intrinsics, extrinsics, and array layouts. To address this, we propose CoIn3D, a generalizable MC3D framework that enables strong transferability from source configurations to unseen target ones. CoIn3D explicitly incorporates all identified spatial priors into both feature embedding and image observation through spatial-aware feature modulation (SFM) and camera-aware data augmentation (CDA), respectively. SFM enriches feature space by integrating four spatial representations, such as focal length, ground depth, ground gradient, and Plücker coordinate. CDA improves observation diversity under various configurations via a training-free dynamic novel-view image synthesis scheme. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoIn3D achieves strong cross-configuration performance on landmark datasets such as NuScenes, Waymo, and Lyft, under three dominant MC3D paradigms represented by BEVDepth, BEVFormer, and PETR.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes CoIn3D, a generalizable framework for multi-camera 3D object detection, addressing spatial prior discrepancies between source and target configurations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to tackle the generalization challenge faced by MC3D models in adapting to unseen multi-camera configurations with varying spatial priors.
AI.method: The framework includes spatial-aware feature modulation (SFM) for enriching feature space and camera-aware data augmentation (CDA) for enhancing observation diversity during training.
AI.result: CoIn3D demonstrates strong cross-configuration performance on multiple datasets (NuScenes, Waymo, Lyft) under prominent MC3D paradigms like BEVDepth, BEVFormer, and PETR.
AI.conclusion: CoIn3D significantly improves transferability across different multi-camera configurations, addressing spatial discrepancies effectively.

title: Risk-Aware Rulebooks for Multi-Objective Trajectory Evaluation under Uncertainty
summary: We present a risk-aware formalism for evaluating system trajectories in the presence of uncertain interactions between the system and its environment. The proposed formalism supports reasoning under uncertainty and systematically handles complex relationships among requirements and objectives, including hierarchical priorities and non-comparability. Rather than treating the environment as exogenous noise, we explicitly model how each system trajectory influences the environment and evaluate trajectories under the resulting distribution of environment responses. We prove that the formalism induces a preorder on the set of system trajectories, ensuring consistency and preventing cyclic preferences. Finally, we illustrate the approach with an autonomous driving example that demonstrates how the formalism enhances explainability by clarifying the rationale behind trajectory selection.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a risk-aware framework for evaluating system trajectories amid uncertain environmental interactions, proving consistency and illustrating it with autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of assessing system trajectories under uncertainty with structured reasoning and explicit environment modeling.
AI.method: Modeling the mutual influence between system trajectories and environments, establishing a preorder to ensure logical consistency.
AI.result: The formalism ensures consistent trajectory evaluation and avoids cyclic preferences.
AI.conclusion: This approach enhances explainability in decision-making by elucidating the rationale behind chosen trajectories, demonstrated through an autonomous driving example.

title: Building AI Coding Agents for the Terminal: Scaffolding, Harness, Context Engineering, and Lessons Learned
comment: Work in progress, new versions will be updated continuously
summary: The landscape of AI coding assistance is undergoing a fundamental shift from complex IDE plugins to versatile, terminal-native agents. Operating directly where developers manage source control, execute builds, and deploy environments, CLI-based agents offer unprecedented autonomy for long-horizon development tasks. In this paper, we present OPENDEV, an open-source, command-line coding agent engineered specifically for this new paradigm. Effective autonomous assistance requires strict safety controls and highly efficient context management to prevent context bloat and reasoning degradation. OPENDEV overcomes these challenges through a compound AI system architecture with workload-specialized model routing, a dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution, lazy tool discovery, and adaptive context compaction that progressively reduces older observations. Furthermore, it employs an automated memory system to accumulate project-specific knowledge across sessions and counteracts instruction fade-out through event-driven system reminders. By enforcing explicit reasoning phases and prioritizing context efficiency, OPENDEV provides a secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance, offering a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OPENDEV, a command-line AI coding assistant designed for long-term development tasks with safety and efficiency measures.
AI.motivation: Shift the paradigm of AI coding assistance from IDE plugins to terminal-native agents for better autonomy in development.
AI.method: OPENDEV uses a compound AI system with workload-specific model routing, dual-agent planning and execution, adaptive context management, lazy tool discovery, and automated memory systems.
AI.result: OPENDEV ensures safe, efficient, and autonomous terminal-first AI coding assistance with enhanced reasoning and memory.
AI.conclusion: OPENDEV provides a secure and extensible blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering, enabling effective terminal-first AI assistance.

title: C2-Faith: Benchmarking LLM Judges for Causal and Coverage Faithfulness in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as judges of chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, but it remains unclear whether they can reliably assess process faithfulness rather than just answer plausibility. We introduce C2-Faith, a benchmark built from PRM800K that targets two complementary dimensions of faithfulness: causality (does each step logically follow from prior context?) and coverage (are essential intermediate inferences present?). Using controlled perturbations, we create examples with known causal error positions by replacing a single step with an acausal variant, and with controlled coverage deletions at varying deletion rates (scored against reference labels). We evaluate three frontier judges under three tasks: binary causal detection, causal step localization, and coverage scoring. The results show that model rankings depend strongly on task framing, with no single judge dominating all settings; all judges exhibit a substantial gap between detecting an error and localizing it; and coverage judgments are systematically inflated for incomplete reasoning. These findings clarify when LLM judges are dependable and where they fail, and provide practical guidance for selecting judges in process-level evaluation

AI.tldr: The study investigates if large language models (LLMs) reliably judge Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning's faithfulness, introducing C2-Faith benchmark to assess causality and coverage dimensions.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used as reasoning judges, but their ability to effectively evaluate reasoning faithfulness, beyond plausible answers, is questionable.
AI.method: The paper uses controlled perturbations from PRM800K to examine judges' abilities in tasks like causal error detection, causal step localization, and coverage scoring.
AI.result: The results reveal task framing highly impacts model rankings, judges struggle with error localization compared to detection, and coverage judgments are inflated for incomplete reasoning.
AI.conclusion: LLMs have limitations in reliably assessing reasoning processes, specifically in detecting and localizing errors, but provide insights for improving process-level evaluations.

title: CLIP-driven Zero-shot Learning with Ambiguous Labels
comment: Accepted by ICASSP 2026 (IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing)
summary: Zero-shot learning (ZSL) aims to recognize unseen classes by leveraging semantic information from seen classes, but most existing methods assume accurate class labels for training instances. However, in real-world scenarios, noise and ambiguous labels can significantly reduce the performance of ZSL. To address this, we propose a new CLIP-driven partial label zero-shot learning (CLIP-PZSL) framework to handle label ambiguity. First, we use CLIP to extract instance and label features. Then, a semantic mining block fuses these features to extract discriminative label embeddings. We also introduce a partial zero-shot loss, which assigns weights to candidate labels based on their relevance to the instance and aligns instance and label embeddings to minimize semantic mismatch. As the training goes on, the ground-truth labels are progressively identified, and the refined labels and label embeddings in turn help improve the semantic alignment of instance and label features. Comprehensive experiments on several datasets demonstrate the advantage of CLIP-PZSL.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes CLIP-PZSL, a framework that improves zero-shot learning (ZSL) under label ambiguity by refining label embeddings and progressively aligning semantics, leading to better performance.
AI.motivation: Addressing the performance degradation of zero-shot learning (ZSL) caused by noisy or ambiguous labels, which are common in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: CLIP-PZSL utilizes a CLIP-driven semantic mining block to extract discriminative label embeddings, employs a partial zero-shot loss to weigh candidate labels' relevance, and progressively refines ground-truth labels for better semantic alignment.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple datasets illustrate that CLIP-PZSL outperforms existing methods in handling label ambiguity.
AI.conclusion: CLIP-PZSL effectively handles label ambiguity in ZSL by leveraging semantic mining, progressive refinement, and partial zero-shot loss, offering an improvement in recognizing unseen classes.

title: Poisoning the Inner Prediction Logic of Graph Neural Networks for Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have achieved remarkable results in various tasks. Recent studies reveal that graph backdoor attacks can poison the GNN model to predict test nodes with triggers attached as the target class. However, apart from injecting triggers to training nodes, these graph backdoor attacks generally require altering the labels of trigger-attached training nodes into the target class, which is impractical in real-world scenarios. In this work, we focus on the clean-label graph backdoor attack, a realistic but understudied topic where training labels are not modifiable. According to our preliminary analysis, existing graph backdoor attacks generally fail under the clean-label setting. Our further analysis identifies that the core failure of existing methods lies in their inability to poison the prediction logic of GNN models, leading to the triggers being deemed unimportant for prediction. Therefore, we study a novel problem of effective clean-label graph backdoor attacks by poisoning the inner prediction logic of GNN models. We propose BA-Logic to solve the problem by coordinating a poisoned node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our method effectively enhances the attack success rate and surpasses state-of-the-art graph backdoor attack competitors under clean-label settings. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BA-Logic

AI.tldr: This paper introduces BA-Logic, a method for clean-label graph backdoor attacks addressing the issue of poisoning the GNN model's prediction logic without modifying training labels. Experimental results show its effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Existing graph backdoor attacks require label alterations which are unrealistic in real-world settings. There is a need for effective clean-label backdoor attacks that work without modifying training labels.
AI.method: The authors propose BA-Logic, which coordinates a poisoned node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator to attack the prediction logic of GNN models under clean-label conditions.
AI.result: BA-Logic outperforms other state-of-the-art graph backdoor attack methods in clean-label settings, achieving a higher attack success rate as demonstrated through experiments on real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: BA-Logic is a novel and effective solution for clean-label graph backdoor attacks, addressing prediction logic poisoning and setting a new benchmark for such attacks.

title: Ailed: A Psyche-Driven Chess Engine with Dynamic Emotional Modulation
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, 11 tables. Open source: https://github.com/chrnx-dev/ailed-chess
summary:
Chess engines passed human strength years ago, but they still don't play like humans. A grandmaster under clock pressure blunders in ways a club player on a hot streak never would. Conventional engines capture none of this.
  This paper proposes a personality x psyche decomposition to produce behavioral variability in chess play, drawing on patterns observed in human games. Personality is static -- a preset that pins down the engine's character. Psyche is dynamic -- a bounded scalar ψ_t \in [-100, +100], recomputed from five positional factors after every move. These two components feed into an audio-inspired signal chain (noise gate, compressor/expander, five-band equalizer, saturation limiter) that reshapes move probability distributions on the fly. The chain doesn't care what engine sits behind it: any system that outputs move probabilities will do. It needs no search and carries no state beyond ψ_t.
  I test the framework across 12,414 games against Maia2-1100, feeding it two probability sources that differ by ~2,800x in training data. Both show the same monotonic gradient in top-move agreement (~20-25 pp spread from stress to overconfidence), which tells us the behavioral variation comes from the signal chain, not from the model underneath. When the psyche runs overconfident, the chain mostly gets out of the way (66% agreement with vanilla Maia2). Under stress, the competitive score falls from 50.8% to 30.1%. The patterns are reminiscent of tilt and overconfidence as described in human play, but I should be upfront: this study includes no human-subject validation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to create human-like behavioral variability in chess engines using a personality and psyche-based model, adding dynamics reminiscent of human errors under pressure.
AI.motivation: Current chess engines surpass humans in skill but lack human-like variability in behavior, such as blunders under pressure, prompting a need for more realistic computational models.
AI.method: Utilizes a personality (static component) and psyche (dynamic factor, ψ_t) decomposition, with an audio-inspired signal chain to reshape move probability distributions in real-time, independent of the engine used.
AI.result: The framework displayed behavioral variability (mimicking stress and overconfidence) using two probability sources, causing a monotonic gradient in top-move agreement and competitive score changes without relying on the underlying model.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates the potential for modeling human-like behavioral traits in chess engines, despite limited real-world validation.

title: Sparse-BitNet: 1.58-bit LLMs are Naturally Friendly to Semi-Structured Sparsity
summary: Semi-structured N:M sparsity and low-bit quantization (e.g., 1.58-bit BitNet) are two promising approaches for improving the efficiency of large language models (LLMs), yet they have largely been studied in isolation. In this work, we investigate their interaction and show that 1.58-bit BitNet is naturally more compatible with N:M sparsity than full-precision models. To study this effect, we propose Sparse-BitNet, a unified framework that jointly applies 1.58-bit quantization and dynamic N:M sparsification while ensuring stable training for the first time. Across multiple model scales and training regimes (sparse pretraining and dense-to-sparse schedules), 1.58-bit BitNet consistently exhibits smaller performance degradation than full-precision baselines at the same sparsity levels and can tolerate higher structured sparsity before accuracy collapse. Moreover, using our custom sparse tensor core, Sparse-BitNet achieves substantial speedups in both training and inference, reaching up to 1.30X.
